title: Comment from Gotwalt, Aaron, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
We write in support of the HTI-5 Proposed Rule and offer one targeted recommendation. We are health IT developers and entrepreneurs with twenty-five years of experience building clinical data exchange and interoperability systems.

ASTP/ONC proposes to expand the definitions of "access" and "use" in § 171.102 to include automated means, including autonomous AI systems. I support this proposal and recommend one addition: clarify that "use" encompasses the automated reconciliation and deduplication of electronic health information from multiple sources.

The Problem

The access problem is substantially solved. FHIR APIs are deployed, TEFCA is operational, and data flows between systems at increasing volume. But a well-documented gap persists between *receiving* data and *using* it.

The Sequoia Project's Data Usability Workgroup (486 participants from 391 organizations) has systematically documented this: duplicate records that cannot be automatically deduplicated, conflicting medication lists across coding systems, and no mechanism for computational resolution. When a physician receives a fifty-page C-CDA from an outside organization, the practical result is that the data is skimmed, ignored, or manually re-entered. KLAS Research confirms that many organizations connected through exchange networks are "not actively consuming outside data." Connection does not equal use.

HTI-5 implicitly acknowledges this by removing the Clinical Information Reconciliation and Incorporation criterion (§ 170.315(b)(2)), which required only manual display-and-select. That criterion failed. But nothing in the current proposal recognizes automated reconciliation as a dimension of "use."

Why It Matters for Information Blocking

An actor that provides technically compliant data, _i.e._ a valid C-CDA or a conformant FHIR response in a format that resists automated reconciliation with competing systems is creating friction that discourages switching and inhibits competition. This is precisely the behavior the Cures Act targeted and that HTI-5's revisions to the Infeasibility Exception (§ 171.204(a)(3)) and Manner Exception (§ 171.301(a)) aim to curtail.

Clarifying that "use" includes reconciliation gives ASTP/ONC an enforcement tool against this pattern. This is consistent with EO 14267 (Reducing Anti-Competitive Regulatory Barriers) and HTI-5's deregulatory posture. It also aligns with the preamble's "FHIR-Forward Future" vision acknowledging that complementary "successor technology or standards" (per the Cures Act, § 170.404) will be needed to provide capabilities FHIR alone does not offer.

Proposed Text

Addition to § 171.102 [new language in brackets]:

> Use means the ability of electronic health information, once accessed or exchanged, to be applied toward its intended purpose. This includes the ability to use electronic health information through automated means, including by autonomous artificial intelligence systems [and by systems that computationally reconcile, deduplicate, or merge electronic health information from multiple sources].

This creates no new certification criteria, mandates no specific technology, and imposes no additional compliance burden on any actor. It provides interpretive clarity for enforcement: practices that unreasonably frustrate automated reconciliation can be evaluated under the information blocking framework.

Access without usability is not meaningful access. We urge ASTP/ONC to make this explicit.

Respectfully submitted,

Aaron Gotwalt (Former CTO of Evernow and Doctrly, San Francisco, CA.)
and
James R. Thompson, D.Phil. (CTO of Morf Health, New York, NY.)
